chosen_title: Perseverance rover finds even more signs of extinct life on Mars
label: SPACE
headline: Signs of Life on Mars
image_prompt: A weathered rust-red boulder in Jezero Crater split open to reveal dark, carbon-streaked interior veins glistening with ancient mineral deposits. The Perseverance rover's drill arm extends into frame from the left, a fine plume of reddish dust still drifting from a fresh bore hole inches deep. Behind the boulder, a fossilized river delta fans across the crater floor under a pale butterscotch sky, conveying immense geological age and silence. A bright accent glow bleeds from the crack in the rock face, casting amber light and deep shadow across the dust-covered ground as if something ancient is illuminated from within. The lower third of the frame is dim, undisturbed flat terrain.
tweets: NASA's Perseverance rover just found a record-breaking trove of organic molecules on Mars — the strongest potential signatures of ancient microbial life ever detected. If this holds up, everything changes. 🧵

Perseverance landed in Jezero Crater in February 2021 — chosen because 3.5 billion years ago it was a river-fed lake. If ancient life ever existed on Mars, a former lakebed is exactly where it would leave chemical traces behind.

What the rover found is described as record-breaking:
• Organic molecules — carbon-based building blocks of life
• Potential signatures of ancient microbial activity
• The greatest concentration of complex organics yet detected on Mars

The key word is 'potential.' Organic molecules can form without biology — meteorites and volcanic chemistry produce them too. What makes this different is the sheer scale and complexity, which is hard to explain without life.

Since 2021, Perseverance has drilled and sealed rock samples in titanium tubes across Jezero Crater. A Mars Sample Return mission would bring them to Earth in the 2030s — where labs could finally confirm whether the chemistry is biological.

If Jezero once hosted microbial life, Mars wasn't always a dead world — and we may not be alone in this solar system.
Follow @decodededge
Source: The Guardian / NASA
